content.text: Platform teams build infrastructure for other engineers — which makes developer experience a first-class product metric. A GenAI platform that is technically correct but frustrating to use will be forked, shadowed, or abandoned. DevEx surveys and structured feedback loops give the platform team a continuous, quantified signal about what is blocking engineers, what is working well, and where to invest. This document covers how to design those surveys, collect complementary signals, and close the feedback loop so contributors see that their input drives change.
Why Platform Teams Need Structured DevEx Measurement

Informal signals — Slack complaints, hallway conversations, the loudest team in the organisation — are biased toward whoever is most vocal. A structured DevEx program surfaces issues from teams that are quietly struggling rather than complaining. It also creates a baseline that makes improvements measurable: without a prior score, there is no way to demonstrate that a documentation rewrite or a faster CI pipeline actually helped.
Counts problems that reach the channel, not problems that engineers give up on
Quarterly survey: 'In the past 3 months, how often did you not pursue a GenAI feature because the platform was too hard to use?'
Under-counts silent failures; over-counts noisy teams
Usage telemetry: feature adoption rate per team segment
Reaches the platform team only when frustration is severe
In-tool micro-survey: one question after an SDK error or a long CI wait
Skewed toward contributors the TC already interacts with
Anonymous survey section with free-text comments
The SPACE Framework for GenAI Platform DevEx

The SPACE framework (Satisfaction, Performance, Activity, Communication & Collaboration, Efficiency) provides a multi-dimensional lens for developer productivity. Applying it to a GenAI platform means mapping each dimension to platform-specific observables.
How developers feel about the platform tools and support
'On a scale of 1–5, how satisfied are you with the GenAI platform tools?'
Quality of outputs produced with platform tools
'How often do GenAI features you build with the platform meet their quality bar on the first production deployment?'
Volume of meaningful work done using the platform
'In the past quarter, how many GenAI features did your team ship using platform libraries?'
Ease of getting help and sharing knowledge
'How easy is it to get a question answered by the platform team within 1 business day?'
'How long does it typically take from starting a new GenAI integration to having it running in production?'

The quarterly survey is the primary structured signal. It must be short (under 10 minutes), consistent across quarters (so trends are meaningful), and anonymous. Anonymity is non-negotiable — engineers will not report that the platform is broken if their name is attached to the response.

# GenAI Platform Quarterly DevEx Survey

**Estimated time: 8 minutes | Responses are anonymous**

## Section 1: Satisfaction (1–5 Likert scale)

1. Overall, how satisfied are you with the GenAI platform tools
   (LLM SDK, prompt registry, eval runner, LLM gateway)?

2. How satisfied are you with the quality of platform documentation?

3. How satisfied are you with the responsiveness of the platform team
   when you have a question or issue?

## Section 2: Efficiency (multiple choice)

4. How long does it typically take to go from a new GenAI feature idea
   to a working prototype using the platform?
   [ ] < 1 day  [ ] 1–3 days  [ ] 4–7 days  [ ] > 1 week

5. How often does a platform tool (SDK, gateway, eval runner, registry)
   block your team's progress for more than 4 hours?
   [ ] Never  [ ] Rarely (<1x/month)  [ ] Sometimes (1–3x/month)  [ ] Often (>3x/month)

## Section 3: Pain points (free text)

6. What is the single biggest friction point when using the GenAI platform?
   (open text, 2–3 sentences)

7. What change to the platform would save your team the most time?
   (open text, 2–3 sentences)

## Section 4: Adoption (multiple choice)

8. Which platform tools does your team actively use? (select all that apply)
   [ ] LLM SDK  [ ] Prompt registry  [ ] Eval runner
   [ ] LLM gateway  [ ] Backstage templates  [ ] Inner-source contributions

9. Are there platform capabilities you need but that don't exist yet?
   [ ] No  [ ] Yes → describe: ___

## Section 5: Net Promoter

10. How likely are you to recommend the GenAI platform to a colleague
    starting a new AI project? (0–10)
All engineers who used a platform tool in the past quarter
Triggered after error or long wait

The quarterly survey captures how developers feel looking back. Micro-surveys capture how they feel in the moment — immediately after an error, after a long CI wait, or after completing a task. The signal is richer and more actionable because it is contextual.
Micro-surveys in the SDK prompt only when the engineer has set
in their environment. This is mentioned in the onboarding golden path but never forced. Surveys that appear without consent are ignored or actively resented — defeating the purpose.

The most important part of a DevEx program is what happens after the survey closes. Engineers who reported problems and see no response stop answering future surveys. Closing the loop means: acknowledging the themes publicly, naming what will and won't be addressed, linking specific fixes to specific survey items, and thanking contributors by name where feedback led to a shipped improvement.

# GenAI Platform — June 2026 DevEx Digest

## What you told us (Q2 survey summary)
- **Top pain point:** SDK documentation examples don't match the current API (38% of respondents)
- **Second pain point:** Eval runner jobs take > 20 minutes for small datasets (27%)
- **Satisfaction score:** 3.6/5 (up from 3.2 in Q1)
- **NPS:** +24 (up from +17 in Q1)

## What we're doing about it

| Feedback theme                          | Action                              | Status   | ETA      |
|-----------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------|----------|
| Docs examples out of date               | Automated doc test in CI            | ✅ Done  | Shipped  |
| Eval runner slow for small datasets     | Fast-path for < 50 rows (no K8s job)| 🔧 WIP   | Jul 2026 |
| Backstage template hard to find         | Added to platform home page         | ✅ Done  | Shipped  |
| No Bedrock adapter                      | Inner-source contribution (thanks Alice!) | ✅ Done | Shipped |

## What we won't address this quarter (and why)
- **Request: Add GPT-4o to standard tier** — Cost governance review required; escalated to PO.
- **Request: Remove DCO requirement** — Legal requirement; cannot be removed.

## Usage highlight
- 47 teams now using the LLM SDK (up from 31 in Q1)
- 12 inner-source contributions merged this quarter

## Next survey
Q3 DevEx survey opens 1 September. It will focus on the eval runner improvements.
Quantitative Metrics to Track Alongside Surveys
Survey scores are self-reported and lagging. Pair them with objective metrics that the platform team can measure continuously.
Median time from first SDK install to first successful LLM call in dev
% of new GenAI services using Backstage template
Median time from PR open to first TC response
Incidents per quarter where the platform tool was the root cause
% of TechDocs pages where developer leaves < 30 seconds after landing
Raw survey responses need structure to be useful. The analysis workflow runs within 2 weeks of the survey closing and produces a concise executive summary and a detailed findings document.

Free-text responses from questions 6 and 7 are clustered into themes. With 50–150 respondents, manual clustering is faster and more reliable than automated topic modelling — a platform team member reads all responses and applies labels.

# analysis/summarise_scores.py
import csv, statistics

scores = {
    "satisfaction":   [],
    "doc_quality":    [],
    "support_responsiveness": [],
    "nps":            [],
}

with open("responses.csv") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        for key in scores:
            val = row.get(key)
            if val and val.isdigit():
                scores[key].append(int(val))

for metric, values in scores.items():
    if not values:
        continue
    mean   = statistics.mean(values)
    median = statistics.median(values)
    stdev  = statistics.stdev(values) if len(values) > 1 else 0
    print(f"{metric:30s}: mean={mean:.2f}, median={median:.1f}, sd={stdev:.2f}, n={len(values)}")

# NPS calculation
nps_scores = scores.get("nps", [])
promoters  = sum(1 for s in nps_scores if s >= 9)
detractors = sum(1 for s in nps_scores if s <= 6)
n          = len(nps_scores)
nps        = (promoters - detractors) / n * 100 if n else 0
print(f"\nNPS: {nps:+.0f} ({promoters} promoters, {detractors} detractors, {n} total)")

Aggregate scores can hide divergent experiences. A satisfaction score of 3.6 across all teams might be 4.5 for teams using only the LLM SDK and 2.2 for teams trying to use the eval runner. Segment the data by team maturity (new adopter / established / power user), by feature set used, and by team size.

# Segment analysis: compare satisfaction by feature-set usage
import csv, collections, statistics

segment_scores: dict[str, list[float]] = collections.defaultdict(list)

with open("responses.csv") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        satisfaction = row.get("satisfaction")
        if not satisfaction or not satisfaction.isdigit():
            continue
        score = int(satisfaction)
        tools = row.get("q8_tools_used", "")
        for tool in ["LLM SDK", "Prompt registry", "Eval runner", "Backstage templates"]:
            if tool.lower() in tools.lower():
                segment_scores[tool].append(score)

print("Satisfaction by tool used:")
for tool, scores in segment_scores.items():
    print(f"  {tool:25s}: {statistics.mean(scores):.2f} (n={len(scores)})")

Not all survey-identified problems can be addressed in the next quarter. The platform team uses an impact × reach × effort scoring model to prioritise. Each dimension is scored 1–3 and the final priority score is
Friction — delays a sprint or causes rework
Blocker — prevents teams from shipping or forces a fork
Multiple teams or a common workflow step
All teams or the critical path (golden path, onboarding)

# Prioritisation model
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class FeedbackItem:
    theme:       str
    mention_count: int
    impact:      int   # 1-3
    reach:       int   # 1-3
    effort:      int   # 1-3 (higher = more effort)

    @property
    def priority_score(self) -> float:
        return (self.impact * self.reach) / self.effort

items = [
    FeedbackItem("Docs examples out of date",           38, impact=2, reach=3, effort=1),
    FeedbackItem("Eval runner slow for small datasets",  27, impact=2, reach=2, effort=2),
    FeedbackItem("No Bedrock adapter",                   18, impact=3, reach=2, effort=2),
    FeedbackItem("Backstage template hard to find",      14, impact=1, reach=3, effort=1),
    FeedbackItem("GPT-4o on standard tier",              11, impact=2, reach=2, effort=3),
]

for item in sorted(items, key=lambda x: x.priority_score, reverse=True):
    print(f"{item.priority_score:.2f}  {item.theme}  (mentions: {item.mention_count})")

# Output (highest priority first):
# 6.00  Docs examples out of date  (mentions: 38)
# 3.00  Backstage template hard to find  (mentions: 14)
# 2.00  Eval runner slow for small datasets  (mentions: 27)
# 3.00  No Bedrock adapter  (mentions: 18)
# 1.33  GPT-4o on standard tier  (mentions: 11)
A DevEx program that runs too many surveys trains engineers to ignore them. Four practices prevent survey fatigue:

The quarterly survey has exactly 10 questions. If a new question is added, an existing one must be retired. Engineers who answered last quarter know the survey takes 8 minutes — that expectation must be reliable.
Every survey invitation links to the previous quarter's digest showing what changed. Engineers who see their feedback drove a shipped fix are far more likely to respond again.

If a quarterly survey is running, micro-surveys are paused for that quarter for respondents who already completed the quarterly. The total survey touchpoints per engineer per quarter should not exceed 2.
Publish the response rate alongside the results — 'Q2 2026: 87/210 engineers responded (41%)'. This signals that the platform team values broad participation and that low response rates are noticed.

DevEx surveys and feedback loops are the mechanism by which a platform team learns whether its tools are actually helping the engineers who use them. A quarterly survey using the SPACE framework captures structured, comparable data across time. In-tool micro-surveys capture contextual friction in the moment. Support ticket themes and usage telemetry provide the quantitative complement to self-reported sentiment. The program only works if the loop is visibly closed — engineers must see that their feedback changes something, or they will stop providing it. A monthly digest that links survey items to shipped fixes is the minimum viable feedback acknowledgement.
